Item.Name: Quercus alba (White Oak)
DescriptionBase: Oval acorns feed wildlife and this large tree provides habitat for birds and shade for humans. Massive, reddish crown adds beauty in the season. Drought and poor soil tolerant.

Item.Name: Quercus bicolor (Swamp White Oak)
DescriptionBase: Large long living tree with a short trunk and spreading oval crown. Leaves are a glossy dark green on top and a silvery white underneath. Blooms golden green catkins in spring.  Leaves turn gorgeous fall colors of gold, red and yellow.
Photo Credits: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Chris Light, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   LocalNatureLab, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons                                                                        Willow, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Quercus palustris (Pin Oak)
DescriptionBase: Broad, pyramidal crown with lower branches that often die off, leaving pin-like stubs. Acorns feed wildlife and leaves turn deep red in the fall.
Photo Credits: By josh jackson - IMG_2887.JPGUploaded by pixeltoo, CC BY 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=7522359

Item.Name: Quercus phellos (Willow Oak)
DescriptionBase: Rounded crown. Leaves turn a coppery orange in the fall. Acorns feed wildlife once the tree matures. Adapted to grow in clay soils. Common landscape tree used for shade.
Photo Credits:
Daderot, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons
Michael Wolf, CC BY-SA 3.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Quercus rubra (Northern Red Oak)
DescriptionBase: Dark green leaves turn brownish-red in fall. Acorns will feed wildlife once tree is mature enough. Drought tolerant.
Photo Credits: By Famartin - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=59086504, Bruce Kirchoff from Greensboro, NC, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Quercus velutina (Black Oak)
DescriptionBase: A medium-sized tree for large yards. Bark is nearly all black on mature trees with deep furrows. Oval acorns feed wildlife.
Shade Program: March 1st- May 1 2026: For sizes valued at $50 or above, a $25 discount can be applied courtesy of the Throwing Shade Virginia program. If you purchase online, this discount will be applied as a refund when you pick up your plants. (see https://rb.gy/lukcmf for details)

Item.Name: Salix nigra (Black Willow)
DescriptionBase: Deciduous fast growing tree prefers moist soils and full sun. Dark brown to black bark, develops deep groves and rough texture with shaggy scales as it ages. Avoid full shade and do not alloy soil to dry out.

Item.Name: Sassafras albidum (Sassafras)
DescriptionBase: Root suckers can make this form multiple trunks and colonies. The leaves turn yellow, purple, and red in the fall. Deer, drought, and clay tolerant.
Photo Credits:
By Pymouss - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=7804970

Item.Name: Taxodium distichum (Bald Cypress)
DescriptionBase: Long lived conifer native to swamps, but also grows well in upland soils. Buttressed bases with knobby "knees" that arise out of standing water. Clay and wet soil tolerant.
Photo Credits: By Thomas & Dianne Jones from Okmulgee, OK, USA - Mt Fork Morning Calm, CC BY 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=11109140

Item.Name: Book
DescriptionBase:
The Plant Ridge & Valley Natives campaign includes the following 11 counties (from north to south): Frederick, Clarke, Warren, Shenandoah, Page, Rockingham, Augusta, Highland, Bath, Rockbridge, and Alleghany. We also include the cities of Winchester, Harrisonburg, Staunton, Waynesboro, Lexington, Buena Vista, and Covington.

In May 2023, we published the first edition of Plant Ridge & Valley Natives: A Gardener�s Guide to Virginia�s Ridge & Valley Native Plants. This full-color, spiral-bound book contains 144 pages of beautiful native plants suitable to using in a home garden or commercial landscape to provide not only beauty, but also high ecological value for our local ecosystems. The Guide includes six garden designs created specifically for us by ecologist Iara Lacher and garden designer Anne Elise Lintelman. 

A second and expanded edition has recently been published. Enlarged to 172 pages, the revised Guide includes an index and glossary, as well as plant lists for gardening challenges such as deer, black walnut, and drought. We have added to the species lists of wildflowers, shrubs, trees, and vines.

Item.Name: Adiantum pedatum (Maidenhair Fern)
DescriptionBase: Deciduous perennial fern with bright green fronds and black stems spreads in a circular pattern. Will do well in shady woodlands, beds, and rain gardens.
Photo Credits:
By Stan Shebs, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=602708,  USFWS - Pacific Region, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
By Walter Siegmund - Own work, CC BY 2.5, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=2127736
By Cephas - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=19729634

Item.Name: Athyrium filix-femina (Lady Fern)
DescriptionBase: Tolerates drier soils than many ferns, but will only tolerate sun if the soil is kept moist. Divide clumps in spring every few years to help spread! Loves shady places!
Photo Credits: Rüdiger [CC BY-SA (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/)]

Item.Name: Dennstaedtia punctilobula (Hay Scented Fern)
DescriptionBase: Crushed leaves release a hay-like scent, giving this fern its name. Fronds turn yellow in the fall. Tolerates dry or poor soils well once established.
Photo Credits: Photo by David J. Stang [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0)]

Item.Name: Dryopteris felix-mas (Male Fern)
DescriptionBase: Large arching deciduous male fern. Erect stout rhizomes and medium green blades. Pinnate-pinnatifid with 20-30 pair of long-pointed pinnae per blade. Leaflet divided into short, rounded, sub-leaflets.
Photo Credits: Agnieszka Kwiecień, Nova, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Dryopteris marginalis (Marginal Woodfern)
DescriptionBase: Evergreen, perennial fern. "Marginal" references the location of the sori on the underside of the pinnae. Grows in a broad, erect crown that is densely covered with light brown scales. Produces a large, single crown and does not spread.
Photo Credits: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, Kelvinsong, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia, Photo by David J. Stang, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons Commons, Laval University, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Matteuccia struthiopteris (Ostrich Fern)
DescriptionBase: Tall bright green plumes turn bronze-gold in fall. In optimal moist conditions will spread to form dense colonies. Deer and rabbit resistant.
Photo Credits:
By de:Benutzer:Griensteidl - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=845398 By The Cosmonaut - Own work This image was created with darktable., CC BY-SA 2.5 ca, Bjoertvedt, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commonshttps://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=89978871
By Photo by David J. Stang - source: David Stang. First published at ZipcodeZoo.com, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=60966557

Item.Name: Onoclea sensibilis (Sensitive Fern)
DescriptionBase: Light green fronds with deep cut lobes are sensitive to first frost in fall. Decorative beaded stalks remain upright in winter. Pale red fiddleheads appear in spring. Rabbit and deer resistant.
Photo Credits:
By peganum - https://www.flickr.com/photos/peganum/7163941421/, CC BY-SA 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=27186100
By Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=78586960
By Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=78586959

Item.Name: Osmunda cinnamomea (Cinnamon Fern)
DescriptionBase: Cinnamon colored fibers near the frond bases give this fern its name. With constant moisture, this shade-loving plant can reach larger sizes.
Photo Credits: Photo by David J. Stang [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0)], Keisotyo, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Osmunda claytoniana (Interrupted Fern)
DescriptionBase: Large vase shaped blueish green fern turns golden yellow in the fall. Silvery-white fiddleheads emerge in spring.
Photo Credits: James St. John, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Osmunda spectabilis (Royal Fern)
DescriptionBase: Tall fern that typically occurs in clumps. Broad fronds with well seperated pinnae. Turns yellow in fall. Naturally occurs on moist bluffs and on ledges along streams.
Photo Credits:
Mason Brock (Masebrock), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons;,  Photo by David J. Stang, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
R. A. Nonenmacher, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Parathelypteris noveboracensis (New York Fern)
DescriptionBase: Clumps usually have 3 or more fronds. Wispy texture due to double cut pinnae. Brown at the base, green once it reaches leaflets. Can form spreading colonies with black trailing rhizomes. Common in woodlands and wetlands.
Photo Credits: Wasp32, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, Laval University, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Polystichum acrostichoides (Christmas Fern)
DescriptionBase: Evergreen fern provides winter color in a landscape. Deer, drought tolerant.
Photo Credits: Photo (c)2006 Derek Ramsey (Ram-Man) [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.5)]

Item.Name: Woodwardia areolata (Netted Chain Fern)
DescriptionBase: Can take considerable sun if the soil is kept wet, but loves shade. Spreads to make colonies in good growing conditions.
Photo Credits: Katja Schultz, Jasper Shide, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Achillea millefolium (Yarrow)
DescriptionBase: This spreading, mat-forming plant can naturalize an area. Fern-like leaves and upright branches lead to bright white-to-pink flowers in late summer. Tolerates poor soils and drought. Deer Resistant.
Photo Credits:
By Pethan, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=525680 
By Dcrjsr - Own work, CC BY 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=16059528

Item.Name: Acorus americanus (Sweet Flag)
DescriptionBase: Flowering with grassy leaves; Ideal for rain gardens and a vigorous spreader. Native to PA, MD, but not VA.

Item.Name: Actaea pachypoda (Dolls Eyes)
DescriptionBase: Blooms small white flowers in clusters during spring. White berries appear in summer which gives the plant its common name as they resemble doll eyes.  Fruits are eaten by a variety of birds.  Plant spreads through rhizomes.
Photo Credits: Meneerke bloem, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,    Laval University, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Actaea racemosa (Black Cohosh)
DescriptionBase: Spears of small, white flowers bloom in early summer and rise well above the rest of the foliage. The plant is known for its odorless insect-repelling properties.
Photo Credits:
By Salicyna - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=47975506
By H. Zell - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=8950508

Item.Name: Agalinis purpurea (Purple False Foxglove)
DescriptionBase: Annual that readily reseeds itself in a garden.  Blooms lovely pink tubular flowers.  Attracts bees.
Photo Credits: Joshua Mayer from Madison, WI, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Agastache nepetoides (Yellow Giant Hyssop)
DescriptionBase: Fast growing perennial that produces long lasting whorls of tiny greenish-yellow flowers. Has large toothed arrow shaped leaves.  Attracts bees and butterflies. Heat and humidity tolerant.
Photo Credits: Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   Potentillarecta, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Agastache scrophulariifolia (Giant Purple Hyssop)
DescriptionBase: Lavender flowers bloom in late summer upon tall spikes, and are a great attractor for bees and butterflies. The strong structure of the flower spikes lets them stay upright through the winter, creating interest in the yard.
Photo Credits: USDA NRCS, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons, Alaina Krakowiak, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Ageratina altissima (White Snakeroot)
DescriptionBase: Tallest aster in its genus.  Booms long lasting white flowers.  Attracts butterflies, bees and songbirds.
Photo Credits: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Allium cernuum (Nodding Onion)
DescriptionBase: Narrow, grass-like leaves lead to small pink flowers the appear in clusters in the summer. Smells of onion when cut. Deer and drought tolerant.

Item.Name: Allium tricoccum (Ramp)
DescriptionBase: Rare delicacies, clipping leaves can add great garlic-like flavor to cooking.
Photo Credits:
Fungus Guy [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0)]
Fungus Guy [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0)]

Item.Name: Amsonia tabernaemontana (Bluestar)
DescriptionBase: Easy to grow plant with a variety of uses. An upright, clump-forming plant with light blue, star-like flowers in late spring. Drought Tolerant. Deer tolerant. Tolerates clay soil.
Photo Credits:
Photo by David J. Stang [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0)]
Sten Porse [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0)]

Item.Name: Anaphalis margaritacea (Pearly Everlasting)
DescriptionBase: Herbaceous perennial or biennial. Long-lasting, yellowish-white flowers bloom in late summer.  Hairy, soft grey stem. Tolerates frost, most soils, and light shade.   Dioecious.  Dried stalks are perfect for floral arrangements.
Photo Credits: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Agnieszka Kwiecień, Nova, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Anemone canadensis (Canada Anemone)
DescriptionBase: Small white flowering plant that forms impressive large colonies through rhizomes.
Photo Credits: John B. (Dendroica cerulea), CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Anemone virginiana (Tall Anemone)
DescriptionBase: Occurring in dry, rocky terrain, this perennial can also thrive in moist, humusly soils. In the spring, 1" five pointed white flowers bloom around a central mound of stamens. Deer, drought, shade tolerant.
Photo Credits: Eric Hunt, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Antennaria neglecta (Field Pussytoes)
DescriptionBase: Small mat forming perennial. Wholly stems, leaves and flower heads. White disk like flowers rise on stalk from basal rosette in spring. Dioecious plant. Females produce seed-like achenes. Attracts butterflies.
Photo Credits:
El Grafo, CC BY-SA 3.0 , via Wikimedia Commons
wackybadger, CC BY-SA 2.0 , via Wikimedia Commons
El Grafo, CC BY-SA 3.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Antennaria parlinii (Parlin's Pussytoes)
DescriptionBase: Small mat forming perennial that blooms fuzzy white flower heads in spring.  Larger blooms compared to other species of pussytoes. Drought and rocky soil tolerant.  Attracts butterflies.
Photo Credits: Aaron Carlson, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, sonnia hill, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Antennaria plantaginifolia (Pussytoes)
DescriptionBase: Soft, gray foliage. Fuzzy, whitish flower heads with pink bloom in the spring. Can spread to form colonies. Drought and rocky soil Tolerant. Attracts butterflies.
Photo Credits: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0)], Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Aplectrum hyemale (Adam and Eve Orchid)
DescriptionBase: Single leaf from late November to March.  Leaves are pin-striped, with alternating silvery-white and green stripes. Late May to early June, flower stalk emerges with flowers
Photo Credits: Allefant, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   Allefant, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,    Choess, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Aquilegia canadensis (Eastern Columbine)
DescriptionBase: Beautiful, red-and-yellow, bell-shaped hanging flowers bloom in spring and attract hummingbirds. Deer and drought tolerant.
Photo Credits: Ragesoss, CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Aralia racemosa (Spikenard)
DescriptionBase: Thicket forming herbacous perennial grows in a shrub like stature.  Dark maroon stem rises above plant blooming green-white flowers that mature to red fruits in fall. Attracts native bees and birds.
Photo Credits: Alex Abair, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Cephas, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Laval University, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Arisaema triphyllum (Jack in the Pulpit)
DescriptionBase: A green and brown hood conceals a "jack" covered in purple flowers in late spring. Flower becomes bright red berries in late summer that feed wildlife. Unique, easy to care for.
Photo Credits: ???? [CC BY-SA 2.5 CA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.5/ca/deed.en)]

Item.Name: Aruncus dioicus (Goatsbeard)
DescriptionBase: Tall, erect bush found in moist woodlands. Plumes of tiny white flowers rise above the foliage in Spring.
Photo Credits:
Agnieszka Kwiecie?, Nova, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons; 
User:Tigerente, CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Asarum canadense (Wild Ginger)
DescriptionBase: Cup-shaped, purple flowers appear in spring grow close to the ground. Great for use as ground cover in shady areas. Deer Tolerant.
Photo Credits: Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Asclepias exaltata (Poke Milkweed)
DescriptionBase: Perennial wildflower plant in the dogbane family.   Species get their name from the Latin word exaltata, meaning “lofty,” due to  the species tall stature.  Requires moist, acid-to-neutral pH soil with high organic content.  Great plant for the boarders of a pollinator garden
Photo Credits: peganum, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, peganum, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, Joshua Mayer from Madison, WI, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Asclepias incarnata (Rose Milkweed)
DescriptionBase: Small, fragrant pink flowers appear in tight clusters at end of summer. Surprisingly tolerant of average soil. Butterfly attractant and supports Monarchs. Deer, Clay, and Wet Soil Tolerant.
Photo Credits: Jasper Shide, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Asclepias purpurascens (Purple Milkweed)
DescriptionBase: Tall milkweed that blooms deep purple and rose pink flowers.  Spreads vigorously through seeds and rhizomes.  Best for naturalized areas.  Drought and deer resistant.  Attracts bees and butterflies.
Photo Credits: Photo by and (c)2009 Derek Ramsey (Ram-Man), GFDL 1.2 <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/old-licenses/fdl-1.2.html>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Asclepias syriaca (Common Milkweed)
DescriptionBase: Milkweed, which native pollinators love, is critically important for monarch butterflies and will vigorously spread to fill a garden.

Item.Name: Asclepias tuberosa (Butterfly Weed Milkweed)
DescriptionBase: Iconic bright, orange flowers are important for monarch butterflies and caterpillars and other pollinators. Drought tolerant.
Photo Credits:
miquelon, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons; 
Ragesoss, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Asclepias verticillata (Whorled Milkweed)
DescriptionBase: Whitish, fragrant flowers bloom in small clusters between June and September. Simple, unbranched stems with long, narrow leaves. Drought and deer tolerant. Attracts butterflies and hummingbirds.
Photo Credits: Mason Brock (Masebrock) [Public domain]

Item.Name: Aster puniceus (Purplestem Aster)
DescriptionBase: Stiff, reddish stems branch into large flower heads. Lavender flowers bloom late in summer. Can spread to form colonies. Attracts butterflies, does well in wet, boggy soils.
Photo Credits: Sarah Johnson, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Baptisia australis (False Blue Indigo)
DescriptionBase: Naturally found in forests along streambanks. Loved for its purple flowers and black seed pods. Perfect for garden or water edges!
Photo Credits: Agnieszka Kwiecień, Nova, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Baptisia tinctoria (Yellow Wild Indigo)
DescriptionBase: Upright, smooth, shrubby perennial. Blooms clusters of small yellow to cream pea-like flowers on terminal racemes from late spring to early summer.

Item.Name: Blephilia ciliata (Downy Wood Mint)
DescriptionBase: Blue-purple flowers emerge in late spring on this pleasant smelling native. Drought tolerant and does well in a wide variety of garden settings.

Item.Name: Blephilia hirsuta (Hairy Wood Mint)
DescriptionBase: Blue-purple flowers appear late in spring to mid-summer in tiered clusters. Fragrant Leaves.

Item.Name: Caltha palustris (Marsh Marigold)
DescriptionBase: Grows well on the edges of ponds and bogs, and can spread rhizomously. Flowers with bright yellow 1-2" blooms in late spring to early summer. Tolerates deer, wet soil.
Photo Credits:
Petar Miloševi?, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, Richard Bartz, Munich aka Makro Freak, CC BY-SA 2.5 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.5>, via Wikimedia Commons
Acabashi, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Campanula americana (Tall Bellflower)
DescriptionBase: Erect biennial wildflower that blooms dark violet star shaped flowers off a single central stem.  Readily reseeds.  A pollinator magnet that attracts butterflies, bees and hummingbirds!
Photo Credits: aspidoscelis, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons, Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons, Quinn Dombrowski from Chicago, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Campanula rotundifolia (Harebell)
DescriptionBase: Rosette forming, upright perennial that readily reseeds and spreads.  Blooms delightful purple bell-shaped flowers.
Photo Credits: janetgraham84new, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  English:  Steve Redman (MORA), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Chamaecrista fasciculata (Partridge Pea)
DescriptionBase: Showy, 1" yellow flowers bloom in late summer in small clusters. Flowers will close when touched. Seed pods mature from green to brown and are eaten by birds. Drought tolerant.
Photo Credits: Fritzflohrreynolds [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0)]

Item.Name: Chelone glabra (White Turtlehead)
DescriptionBase: Clump forming perennial that blooms late summer to fall. White two-lipped flowers with a tinge of pink on spike like terminal racemes. Lance-shaped dark green leaves. Attracts pollinators and hummingbirds.
Photo Credits:
Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 , via Wikimedia Commons
Superior National Forest, CC BY 2.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Chrysopsis mariana (Maryland Golden Aster)
DescriptionBase: Short lived perennial aster that readily reseeds itself.  Produces long blooming bright yellow daisy-like flowers.  Drought resistant. Attracts butterflies and bees.
Photo Credits: Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Masebrock, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Conoclinium coelestinum (Mistflower)
DescriptionBase: Can spread aggressively by rhizomes. Purplish stems with showy, fluffy, tubular, blue-purple flowers in late summer to early fall. Attracts butterflies.
Photo Credits: Kingdon [Public domain], Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Coreopsis lanceolata (Lanceleaf Coreopsis)
DescriptionBase: Showy yellow flowers in this drought tolerant, easy to grow plant. Spreads quickly to fill a space.
Photo Credits: Forest & Kim Starr, CC BY 3.0 US <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/us/deed.en>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Coreopsis tripteris (Tall Coreopsis)
NativeRange: Valley & Ridge, Nova, Piedmont, Coastal Plain
DescriptionBase: Tall, upright, multi-branching plant with 2" yellow flowers that bloom in late summer and fall attract butterflies and make excellent cuttings! Deer Tolerant and Drought Tolerant.
Photo Credits: Frank Mayfield from Chicago area, USA [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0)]

Item.Name: Coreopsis verticillata (Threadleaf Coreopsis)
DescriptionBase: Thrives in poor, rocky, dry soils! Bright yellow flowers bloom in summer. Deer and Drought Resistant.
Photo Credits: User:Qwertzy2 [CC BY-SA (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/)], Photo by David J. Stang, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Corydalis sempervirens (Pale Corydalis)
DescriptionBase: Distinct, pale, multi-lobed leaves distinguish the growth of this perennial until early summer, when it shoots up stems of delicate, 1/2 inch pink-and-yellow flowers. Biennial but will self-seed to colonize.
Photo Credits: Laval University, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Delphinium exaltatum (Tall Larkspur)
DescriptionBase: Tall spikes of 1" blue flowers bloom in summer, attracting butterflies. Grows taller and blooms later than many other larkspurs. Remove spent flowers to encourage new blooms. Deer tolerant.
Photo Credits: Doppelbrau, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, Photo by David J. Stang, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Dicentra cucullaria (Dutchmans breeches)
DescriptionBase: In Spring basal fern-like leaves appear with a leafless arching stem of fragrant white pantaloon-shaped flowers. Adds early interest to the garden.  Plant goes dormant by summer.
Photo Credits: Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons,  Doug McGrady from Warwick, RI, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Dodecatheon amethystinum (Amethyst Shooting Star)
DescriptionBase: Fom April to May this spectacular flower produce fragrant purple to pink colored blossoms.   Rare to find in the wild.  Naturally found in very limited native range including a small area of the Appalachians.    Fruit is an erect, oval to cylindric capsule and mature in late summer.
Photo Credits: wackybadger, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Dodecatheon meadia (Midland Shooting Star)
DescriptionBase: Herbaceous perennial that forms basal rosettes.  In spring a tall red or green stem rises above the basal rosette and blooms unique pink or white flowers that resemble the shape of a shooting star.
Photo Credits: Björn S..., CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, H. Zell, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Erigeron pulchellus (Robin's Plantain)
DescriptionBase: White, aster-like flowers bloom in late spring on sparsley-leaved stems. Tolerates drought and clay soil. Attracts butterflies.
Photo Credits: Salicyna [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0)], Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Eryngium yuccifolium (Rattlesnake Master)
DescriptionBase: Long, sword-like leaves with domed flower clusters. Whitish flower heads appear in summer, Drought, clay, rocky-soil tolerant.
Photo Credits: Sesamehoneytart [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0)], Crazytwoknobs, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Erythronium americanum (Yellow Trout Lily)
DescriptionBase: Spring ephemeral.  Blooms a single beautiful, delicate yellow flower on a stalk. At least two leaves at its base. Forms colonies. Can take 4 to 5 years to bloom. Support Miner Bees.
Photo Credits: Doug McGrady from Warwick, RI, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   Cephas, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via , Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia CommonsWikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Eupatorium hyssopifolium (Hyssop-Leaved Boneset)
DescriptionBase: Dense white flower clusters emerge in the summer. Does well in dry meadows and gravelly soil. Attracts butterflies and native bees.
Photo Credits: By Choess - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=11455131

Item.Name: Eupatorium perfoliatum (Common Boneset)
DescriptionBase: White flowers with grassy leaves, and a vigorous spreader. Ideal for rain gardens.
Photo Credits: I, SB Johnny, CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons, H. Zell, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Eupatorium purpureum (Sweet Joe Pye Weed)
DescriptionBase: Large plants with purple blossoms can be showy additions to a garden. Attracts butterflies, birds, and pollinators. Deer resistant.
Photo Credits: Tatters ❀ from Brisbane, Australia, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Eupatorium serotinum (Late Boneset)
DescriptionBase: Clusters of small white flowers attract pollinators in late summer and fall that attract a variety of pollinators. Can form colonies through rhizomes.
Photo Credits: Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, Melissa McMasters from Memphis, TN, United States, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Eurybia macrophylla (Big Leaved Aster)
DescriptionBase: Large, 4-8 leaves give this plant its name, and is often used for its foliage rather than its flowering, especially if grown in shade. Pale blue flowers with yellow centers bloom in late summer, especially in partial shade.
Photo Credits:
By wackybadger - https://www.flickr.com/photos/wackybadger/6170898173/, CC BY-SA 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=17350819, Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
By User:BotBln - Own work: User:BotBln, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=16453405

Item.Name: Eurybia spectabilis (Showy Aster)
DescriptionBase: Flowers, up to 2" wide, bloom for a long time in late summer and fall. Attracts butterflies and bees. Can form small colonies from underground rhizomes.

Item.Name: Euthamia graminifolia (Grass leaved Goldenrod)
DescriptionBase: A goldenrod with grass-like leaves and soft yellow flowers.  Flowers have a long bloom season from late summer to early winter and are favored by our native bees. Spreads by rhizomes to form attractive clumps.
Photo Credits: Cephas, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Joshua Mayer from Madison, WI, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Eutrochium fistulosum (Hollow Joe Pye Weed)
DescriptionBase: Erect, clump-forming flower with a long bloom.  Large, fragrant,pink rounded clusters of flowers.  Great planting for attracting bees and butterflies!
Photo Credits: Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons,  Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons,  Ryan Hodnett, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   Doug McGrady from Warwick, RI, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Eutrochium maculatum (Joe Pye Weed)
DescriptionBase: Tall herbaceous perennial that bloom dusky pink flowers. Fragrant and showy.  Can form small colonies that can be divided in spring.  Drought and deer resistant. Attracts butterflies.
Photo Credits: Marc Ryckaert, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia, Cephas, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons Commons

Item.Name: Filipendula rubra (Queen of the prairie)
DescriptionBase: Very tall, upright, clump-forming perennial, wide panicles of tiny, fragrant, pale pink flowers in early to mid summer. Bright green fragrant leaves.Valued for both its leaves and its flowers.
Photo Credits: Uleli, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, Bernard Spragg. NZ from Christchurch, New Zealand, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Fragaria virginiana (Wild Strawberry)
DescriptionBase: Herbaceous, flowering, perennial.  Spread by runners making it a great option for ground cover. Produce large colonies over time. White flowers in spring make way for bright red edible fruits. Most productive in color temperatures of spring and fall.
Photo Credits: Walter Siegmund, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Laval University, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  James St. John, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Gentianella quinquefolia (Stiff Gentian)
DescriptionBase: Biennial wildflower that in its second year produces purple funnel-shaped flowers.  Attracts native bees.
Photo Credits: Alex Abair, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Masebrock, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons,  Joshua Mayer from Madison, WI, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Geranium maculatum (Wild Geranium)
DescriptionBase: Bright saucer-shaped purple flowers bloom in spring. Can naturalize and spread in optimal growing conditions, so can be used as a ground cover.
Photo Credits:
Eric in SF, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
By H. Zell - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=10241148

Item.Name: Goodyera pubescens (Downy Rattlesnake Plantain)
DescriptionBase: Evergreen herbaceous perennial in the orchid family. Leaf markings resemble the skin of rattlesnakes.  Flower stalks are covered with down. Blooms racemes of white flowers.  May take several years before blooming. Pollinated by bees.
Photo Credits: Jason Hollinger, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   NC Orchid from North Carolina, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   Joshua Mayer from Madison, WI, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Helenium autumnale (Autumn Helenium)
DescriptionBase: Upright perennial that loves watery landscape. 2" yellow flowers with wedge-shaped petals bloom in late summer. Deer Resistant. Tolerates Clay.

Item.Name: Helianthus angustifolius (Narrow-Leaf Sunflower)
DescriptionBase: Big (2-3'), numerous yellow flowerheads bloom in Fall and continue to frost, where seeds feed wildlife. Great cut flowers! Attracts butterflies and birds. Deer resistant.

Item.Name: Heliopsis helianthoides (False Oxeye Sunflower)
DescriptionBase: This small sunflower relative provides pollinators with cheery yellow blooms in late summer.
Photo Credits: Stefan.lefnaer, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Hepatica nobilis acuta (Sharp lobed hepatica)
DescriptionBase: Pink, white or purple blossoms in early spring.  Fuzzy stems.  Leaves grow from ground level and appear after the flowers have bloomed. Evergreen until following spring.
Photo Credits: Robert Flogaus-Faust, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Heuchera americana (American Alum Root)
DescriptionBase: Small, mounding herbaceous perennial.  Known for their colorful leaves that can be green or variegated in shades of purple, bronze or cream. Bell shaped flowers emerge in the spring on leafless stalks above the foliage.
Photo Credits: Raul654, CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Salvor Gissurardottir, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons,  Doug McGrady from Warwick, RI, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,

Item.Name: Heuchera villosa (Hairy Alumroot)
DescriptionBase: Hairy, three-lobed green leaves with a velvety texture and hairy, brown flowering stems. Late summer blooms of whitish flowers.
Photo Credits:
FD Richards https://www.flickr.com/photos/50697352@N00/21449831956
By Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=53104610
By Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=55531552

Item.Name: Hexastylis arifolia (Little Brown Jug)
DescriptionBase: Clump forming. Evergreen perennial. When exposed to cold weather green leaves may turn a purple hue. Named after its brownish-red jug shaped flowers that bloom from March to June. Likes shade but does best in dappled shade.
Photo Credits: Photo by David J. Stang, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, sonnia hill, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Hibiscus laevis (Halberdleaf Rose-mallow)
DescriptionBase: Large 4-6" diameter white flowers emerge in July. Attracts butterflies
Photo Credits: Eric Hunt, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Hibiscus moscheutos (Swamp Rose Mallow)
DescriptionBase: Large 4-6" diameter white flowers emerge from July to December. Attracts butterflies

Item.Name: Hypercium hypercoides (St Andrew's Cross)
DescriptionBase: This perennial sub-shrub likes acidic soils. Bright, yellow, four-petaled flowers form a cross and bloom in summer. Drought Tolerant
Photo Credits: Peter Friedman

Item.Name: Impatiens capensis (Jewelweed)
DescriptionBase: These annuals can spread well through self seeding. Bright orange flowers attract hummingbirds and butterflies when they bloom through the summer. Great for boggy areas and rain gardens.
Photo Credits: Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 , via Wikimedia Commons,  sonnia hill, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Iris cristata (Dwarf Crested Iris)
DescriptionBase: Low growing, rapidly spreading perennial. Blooms in spring. Produce pale blue lilac flowers with three parallel golden crested ridges on sepals. Narrow sword-shaped yellowish-green leaves and short stalks.
Photo Credits:
Ryan Somma, CC BY 2.0 , via Wikimedia Commons
Drew Avery, CC BY 2.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Iris versicolor (Blue Flag)
DescriptionBase: Native iris with charismatic, purple flowers.
Photo Credits: No machine-readable author provided. Dlanglois assumed (based on copyright claims)., CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Kosteletzkya virginica (Virginia Saltmarsh Mallow)
DescriptionBase: Small pink flowers in summer attract butterflies. This plant does well around water edges and in well-irrigated soil.
Photo Credits: peganum from Henfield, England, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Liatris scariosa (Eastern Blazing Star)
DescriptionBase: Tall perennial that likes moist soils. Spikes of deep purple flower clusters bloom in late summer and attract birds and butterflies to feed. Clay and Drought Tolerant.

Item.Name: Liatris scariosa var. nieuwlandii (Northern Blazing Star)
DescriptionBase: Purple, 1" flowers grow in columned bunches attract bees and butterflies in late summer. Loves sandy, rocky soils.

Item.Name: Liatris spicata (Blazing Star)
DescriptionBase: Tall perennial that likes moist soils. Spikes of deep purple flower clusters bloom in late summer and attract birds and butterflies to feed. Clay and Drought Tolerant.

Item.Name: Lilium superbum (Turk's Cap Lily)
DescriptionBase: This tall lily is noted for the spectacular, orange flowers that bloom in mid-summer, attracting butterflies.
Photo Credits:
By Uleli - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=15859116
By Plantlady - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=27510605

Item.Name: Lobelia cardinalis (Cardinal Flower)
DescriptionBase: This native, red flower is a favorite of hummingbirds and makes an excellent cut flower as well!
Photo Credits: Barnes, Dr. Thomas G., Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Lobelia inflata (Indian Tobacco)
DescriptionBase: Not a true tobacco. Hairy stem with pointed leaves with white-purple flowers that attract small bees. Toxic to mammals, so deer resistant. An annual, but reseeds itself
Photo Credits: H. Zell [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0)]

Item.Name: Lobelia siphilitica (Blue Lobelia)
DescriptionBase: This showy blue flower creates late summer blooms that attract birds, butterflies, and is of special value to bumblebees.
Photo Credits: Cephas, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Lobelia spicata (Pale Spiked Lobelia)
DescriptionBase: Erect annual that readily reseeds. The flower is a spike-like cluster of small pale purplish-blue to bluish-white flowers. Capsule like fruits emerge in late fall. Ideal for a meadow planting.
Photo Credits: Joshua Mayer from Madison, WI, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Ludwigia alternifolia (Seedbox)
DescriptionBase: This yellow-flowering native blooms in summer, and loves marshes and wet meadows. Distinct square, seedpods give this plant its name.
Photo Credits:
By Fritzflohrreynolds - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=31404456
Anita Gould https://www.flickr.com/photos/anitagould/135945901/in/faves-carol_simmons/

Item.Name: Lupinus perennis (Wild Lupine)
DescriptionBase: Pea-like blue and purple flowers. Important food source for the caterpillar of several species of threatened butterflies.
Photo Credits:
By cassi saari - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=72995600By Steven Katovich, USDA Forest Service, Bugwood.org http://www.forestryimages.org/browse/subthumb.cfm?sub=5961#?sub=5961&aut=1552, CC, DouglasGoldman, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
By 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=42011671By Richard Carter, Valdosta State University, Bugwood.org - http://www.forestryimages.org/browse/subthumb.cfm?sub=5961#?sub=5961&aut=11916, CC BY 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=42011984

Item.Name: Melanthium virginicum (Bunchflower)
DescriptionBase: Blooms large clusters of fragrant flowers in summer. Flowers bloom white to cream in color but over time they turn green, then maroon, and finally black.  Wonderful fall interest for a garden or prairie!
Photo Credits: Lee Casebere. USDA NRCS. 1995. Northeast wetland flora: Field office guide to plant species. Northeast National Technical Center, Chester. Courtesy of USDA NRCS Wetland Science Institute., Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons,   Uleli, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Mertensia virginica (Virginia Bluebell)
DescriptionBase: Early spring flower that is a treasure of Virginia! Short blooming, but grows into colonies that blanked the forest floor
Photo Credits:
Jarek Tuszy?ski, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons;,  Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons
Khan "Sadh" N. Mostafa, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons; Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Mimulus ringens (Monkey Flower)
DescriptionBase: This plant does well in wet soils and swampy areas. 1" purple flowers appear in pairs resemble the face of a smiling monkey and bloomin summer. Deer tolerant.
Photo Credits: Ksd5, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Mitchella repens (Partridge Berry)
DescriptionBase: This native ground cover will naturalize an area through root notes. Small white flowers lead to bright red berries that ripen in summer and can persist through winter. Tolerates dry soil and heavy shade.
Photo Credits: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons; Jomegat, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons; jillllybean, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Monarda didyma (Scarlet Beebalm)
DescriptionBase: Fragrant leaves are still used to make teas and salads. Tubular, two-lipped bright red flowers grow in clusters atop long stems and bloom in mid to late summer. Attracts bees, hummingbirds, butterflies. Deer and clay tolerant.
Photo Credits: H. Zell [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0)], Alex Abair, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Monarda fistulosa (Wild Bergamot)
NativeRange: Valley & Ridge, Blue Ridge, Nova, Piedmont
DescriptionBase: This mint-family plant has light-purple flowers that blossom in late summer, which attract bees, hummingbirds, and butterflies. Tolerates some drought and clay soils. Deer Resistant.
Photo Credits:
By Hardyplants - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=95018400
By gailhampshire from Cradley, Malvern, U.K - Monarda fistulosa ?, CC BY 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=49985316 ,  Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Monarda punctata (Spotted Beebalm)
DescriptionBase: Yellow flowers spotted with purple in mid summer. The aromatic, oblong leaves may be used in teas. Long summer bloom period. Attracts butterflies. Drought Tolerant.
Photo Credits: Amber M. King, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Oenothera biennis (Evening Primrose)
DescriptionBase: Bright yellow flowers open at dusk and close again in the morning when hit by sun.  Highly drought tolerant. Fruits are capsules.  Die after fruiting but seeds sprout the following year.
Photo Credits: Christian Ferrer, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons, Pmau, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Oligoneuron rigidum (Stiff leaved Goldenrod)
DescriptionBase: Blooms tiny, bright yellow flowers on dense flat-topped terminal clusters from late summer to early fall.  Individual flowers are larger than those of other native goldenrods.  Hairy broad-leaved stems.
Photo Credits: Daniel McClosky, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Cephas, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Opuntia humifusa (Virginian Prickly Pear)
DescriptionBase: This plant is the only cactus of the easter US! Bright, stunning, yellow flowers bloom in early summer on this cactus, giving way to edible fruits. Grows in clumps up to 3' across.

Item.Name: Osmorhiza claytonii (Sweet Cicely)
DescriptionBase: Perennial wildflower with hairy dark green fernlike leaves and small white flowers.  Attracts butterflies.  Great planting for a woodland garden.
Photo Credits: Jennifer Anderson. United States, IA, Scott Co., Davenport. 2001., Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons,  Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Packera aurea (Golden Ragwort)
DescriptionBase: This striking, shade loving ground cover spreads quickly by self-seeding and adds a long spring bloom of bright, yellow daisy-like flowers.

Item.Name: Packera obovata (Roundleaf Groundsel)
DescriptionBase: Blooms long lasting yellow daisy-like flowers from spring to early summer. Flowers atop sparsely leaved stem that rises from basal clump of serrate, rounded leaves that taper at the end. Attracts butterflies.
Photo Credits: SB_Johnny | PA!, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, sonnia hill, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Parthenium integrifolium (Wild Quinine)
DescriptionBase: Clump forming perennial naturally found in dry soils of prairies, glades and rocky woods. Bloom white wolly looking flowers from May to August. Rough fragrant leaves with toothed margins.

Item.Name: Pedicularis canadensis (Wood Betony)
DescriptionBase: Herbaceous perennial with fern like leaves and silvery hairs.  Blooms bright yellow and red flower clusters on a singular stalk.  Attracts bumblebees.
Photo Credits: DouglasGoldman, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Doug McGrady from Warwick, RI, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Penstemon digitalis (Foxglove White Beardtongue)
DescriptionBase: White flowers adorn tall, woody stalks on this lovely addition to forest edges or clearings.
Photo Credits: Eric Hunt, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Penstemon hirsutus (Hairy Beardtongue)
DescriptionBase: Lovely trumpet-shaped, lavender flowers that bloom late May through June attract a variety of pollinators. Deer resistant!
Photo Credits: Agnieszka Kwiecień, Nova, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Penstemon laevigatus (Eastern Smooth Beardtongue)
DescriptionBase: Erect herbaceous perennial with a large basal rosette and panicle of tubular purple flowers. Prefers wetter soils compared to other penstemon species.  Attracts bees butterflies and hummingbirds.
Photo Credits: Becky Dillhttps://www.inaturalist.org/people/gillydilly, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons, Michael Wolf, Penig, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Penstemon pallidus (Pale Beardtongue)
DescriptionBase: Earlier blooming penstemon covered with soft white hairs.  Blooms tubular light purple flowers.  Attracts native bees. Drought and deer resistant.
Photo Credits: https://www.inaturalist.org/people/supertiger, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Francis Groeters, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Phlox divaricata (Wild Blue Phlox)
DescriptionBase: This spreading wildflower can form mats, thriving in woodland shade. Groups of star shaped flowers bloom in spring, creating striking carpets of color.
Photo Credits:
Photo by and (c)2007 Derek Ramsey (Ram-Man), CC BY-SA 2.5 , via Wikimedia Commons, Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons
CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=192105

Item.Name: Phlox maculata (Wild Sweet William)
DescriptionBase: Upright flower with fragrant, pink, star-shaped flowers that attract hummingbirds and bloom in the summer. Prefers good air circulation and moist soils. Deer resistant.
Photo Credits: Robert H. Mohlenbrock. USDA NRCS. 1995. Northeast wetland flora: Field office guide to plant species. Northeast National Technical Center, Chester. Courtesy of USDA NRCS Wetland Science Institute. -

Item.Name: Phlox paniculata (Garden Phlox)
DescriptionBase: This taller phlox produces fragrant, pink-purple blooms in late summer that attract butterflies and hummingbirds. Deer, clay tolerant
Photo Credits: Photo by David J. Stang, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Phlox stolonifera (Creeping Phlox)
DescriptionBase: Fragrant, tubular, lavender flowers emerge in spring. Can spread to form colonies in good conditions, making this an excellent ground cover. Drought, deer tolerant.
Photo Credits:
Photo by David J. Stang, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, Photo by and (c)2007 Derek Ramsey (Ram-Man), GFDL 1.2 <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/old-licenses/fdl-1.2.html>, via Wikimedia Commons
Jerzy Opio?a, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Physostegia virginiana (Obedient Plant)
DescriptionBase: Tubular, two-lipped pale-purple flower spikes bloom through summer and attract hummingbirds. Each individual flower will temporarily be pushed into a new position! Tolerates Deer and Clay Soil.

Item.Name: Podophyllum peltatum (May Apple)
DescriptionBase: One of the first herbacous perennials to emerge in spring.  Has one to two umbrella like leaves and blooms a singular white flower at the axil of the leaves. Produces a small golden yellow edible fruit.  Plant goes dormant in the summer.  Forms colonies through rhizomes.
Photo Credits: Barnes Dr Thomas G, U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons,  Famartin, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Valis55, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Polemonium reptans (Jacobs Ladder)
DescriptionBase: This low growing wildflower thrives in rich, moist, partially-shaded soil where it can self-seed. Light blue bell-shaped flowers appear in late spring.
Photo Credits: By Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=76903241, Alex Abair, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Pseudognaphalium obtusifolium (Sweet Everlasting)
DescriptionBase: White, conical flowerheads bloom in summer. Whitish hairs cover the surface of the upper stems.
Photo Credits:
By H. Zell - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=10957744, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
By BeckyLaboy - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=98494610

Item.Name: Pycnanthemum muticum (Clustered Mountain Mint)
DescriptionBase: Clump forming perennial can spread by rhizomes. Short, densly clustered leaves have a strong mint aroma. Tubular, pink flowers top plants in mid to late summer and attract butterflies and bees.
Photo Credits:
I, SB Johnny, CC BY-SA 3.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, peganum from Henfield, England, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Pycnanthemum tenuifolium (Narrow Leafed Mountain Mint)
DescriptionBase: This is a vigorous grower and will spread well. Narrow leaves emit a mint-like scent when crushed and is uses in teas. Small white flowers that bloom in mid summer. Tolerates Drought, Clay soils.
Photo Credits: By Fritzflohrreynolds - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=31611887, Alex Abair, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Pycnanthemum verticillatum (Hairy Mountain Mint)
DescriptionBase: Easy to care for once established, this clump forming mind grows through rhizomes. Small, light-purple flowers attract bees, butterflies. Leaves give a strong mint aroma.
Photo Credits: Anne McCormack https://www.flickr.com/photos/mccormacka/19355550105, Alan Weakley, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Pycnanthemum virginianum (Virginia Mountain Mint)
DescriptionBase: This aromatic native is a true mint with delicate white flowers and can be used to make tea! Vigorous grower and deer resistant!
Photo Credits: Hardyplants at English Wikipedia, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Rhexia virginica (Meadow Beauty)
DescriptionBase: Water loving perennial that spreads rapidly through tubers.  Taller plants sprawl along the ground while shorter ones stand erect.  Blooms bubblegum pink flowers with striking yellow anthers.  Red seed capsules add winter interest in the garden.
Photo Credits: Doug McGrady from Warwick, RI, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Rudbeckia fulgida (Orange Coneflower)
DescriptionBase: Clump-forming coneflower with yellow, daily-like flowers in mid-summer to fall. Prefer consistent moisture until established, then drought tolerant. Deer tolerant. Dry/rocky soil tolerant.

Item.Name: Rudbeckia hirta (Black Eyed Susan)
DescriptionBase: One of the most popular native flowers, Black-Eyed Susans spread over open fields and attract butterflies. Deer Resistant!

Item.Name: Rudbeckia laciniata (Cut-Leaved Coneflower)
DescriptionBase: Cheery, yellow flowers stand tall and attract butterflies, bees, and some birds. Also makes great cut flowers.
Photo Credits: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Rudbeckia triloba (Brown Eyed Susan)
DescriptionBase: Densely branched short-lived perennial will self-seed. Dyellow, daisy-like flowers bloom densely in the plant from summer to fall. Attracts butterflies. Deer resistant.
Photo Credits: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Ruellia humilis (Low Wild Petunia)
DescriptionBase: Tubular, bell-shaped lavender flowers bloom May to October. Oblong to lancelike olive green leaves. Hairy stems and leaves. Attracts butterflies.
Photo Credits:
peganum from Small Dole, England, CC BY-SA 2.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, Alvesgaspar, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
Leonora (Ellie) Enking from East Preston, United Kingdom, CC BY-SA 2.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Ruellia strepens (Smooth Wild Petunia)
DescriptionBase: Clump-forming perennial with pale lilac to lavender petunia like flowers. Flowers bloom May to September. Tolerate close to full shade.

Item.Name: Sagittaria latifolia (Broad-leaved Arrowhead)
DescriptionBase: Found in marshes, swamps and bogs this plant prefers standing water or moist soils.  Blooms three petaled white flowers and underground starchy tubers that are eatten by ducks and turtles.
Photo Credits: Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   Ryan Hodnett, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Salvia lyrata (Lyre-Leaved Sage)
DescriptionBase: This perennial prefers sandy soils and clays. Two-lipped, purple flowers bloom in late spring and attract bees and butterflies. Can self-seed to naturalize an area. Deer resistant.
Photo Credits: Kaldari, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Sanguinaria canadensis (Bloodroot)
DescriptionBase: Bright, short-lived, white, 2" flowers bloom in early spring. Spreads to form colonies through rhizomes. Goes dormant in mid-to-late summer. Reddish-orange sap "bleeds" when plant is cut.

Item.Name: Saururus cernuus (Lizard's Tail)
DescriptionBase: Water loving perennial that gets its name for their long racemes of white flowers.  Roots and foliage smell of citris and sassafrass. Form large colonies along waterways.
Photo Credits: Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Sedum pulchellum (Widow's Cross)
DescriptionBase: Readily reseeding annual often found in rocky outcroppings.  Pink flowers with black and red dotted petals. Lime green succulent-like leaves.
Photo Credits: Mason Brock (Masebrock), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons,   Thomas R. Koll, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Sedum ternatum (Woodland Stonecrop)
DescriptionBase: Clusters of tiny white, star-like flowers with purplish stamens appear on stems above the foliage in spring. Rabbit, deer and drought tolerant.
Photo Credits: By Photo by David J. Stang - source: David Stang. First published at ZipcodeZoo.com, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=61182287,   Fritz Flohr Reynolds from USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Senna hebecarpa (Wild Senna)
DescriptionBase: Bright, yellow flowers form in dense clusters bloom in summer to attract pollinators and butterflies. Black seed pods emerge in fall and support wildlife.
Photo Credits: Small Dole, England [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0)], peganum from Small Dole, England, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Silene caroliniana (Wild Pink)
DescriptionBase: This showy, mat-forming plant boasts clusters of bright pink, star-shaped flowers that bloom early spring.
Photo Credits:
Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 , via Wikimedia Commons
Amadej Trnkocsy: https://www.flickr.com/photos/atrnkoczy/12274247696

Item.Name: Silene stellata (Starry Campion)
DescriptionBase: Clump forming perennial with wide branching panicles of frilly white flowers.  Whorled lance shaped leaves. Attracts butterflies, bumblebees and moths.
Photo Credits: English:  NPS, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons,  Alex Abair, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Silene virginica (Fire Pink)
DescriptionBase: This clump forming perennial grows in rocky, wooded slopes and prefer sandy or gravelly soils. Five pointed, 2" red flowers with notched tips bloom in spring and can spread by self-seeding. Drought, clay tolerant
Photo Credits: Smithsonian American Art Museum, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons Photo by and (c)2008 Derek Ramsey (Ram-Man). Co-attribution must be given to the Chanticleer Garden., CC BY-SA 3.0 , via Wikimedia, peganum from Small Dole, England, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons Commons

Item.Name: Sisyrinchium angustifolium (Narrowleaf Blue-eyed Grass)
DescriptionBase: Blue flowers with grass-like foliage. Not actually a grass but in the iris family! Sword-shaped leaves form a fan shape similar to other plants in the iris family. Grow from rhizomes.
Photo Credits: Agnieszka Kwiecie?, Nova, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, Laval University, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Smallanthus uvedalius (Bear's Foot)
DescriptionBase: Herbaceous perennial in the Asteraceae (daisy) family. Large lobed leaves cup the stem. Shape of the leaf gives it the name of bear's foot. Naturally found growing in woodland edges, meadows, along streams, and open woods. Native bees hibernate in stems during winter.

Item.Name: Solidago caesia (Blue stemmed Goldenrod)
DescriptionBase: Perennial that grows 3 feet tall.  Bloom showy clusters of yellow flowers in summer and early fall that hug greenish-purple stems. Does not spread as rapidly or aggressively as some other goldenrods.
Photo Credits: Eric Hunt, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Solidago canadensis (Common Solidago/Goldenrod)
DescriptionBase: Late summer to early fall blooms of abundant, small, bright yellow flowers. A wonderful plant for native bees and butterflies. Rhizomes can help this plant colonize an area.
Photo Credits: Harry Rose from South West Rocks, Australia [CC BY (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0)], Cephas, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Solidago flexicaulis (Zigzag Solidago)
DescriptionBase: Yellow flowers bloom from mid-summer to fall and are attractive to bees and butterflies. Plants may be grown by seed and can self-seed in the garden. Deer, heavy shade and clay soil tolerant.
Photo Credits: By Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=76407376, Cephas, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Solidago juncea (Early Goldenrod)
DescriptionBase: Tiny bright yellow flowers, provides good color and contrast for late summer and early fall. A wonderful plant for native bees and butterflies. Tolerates clay soil, drought, and deer.

Item.Name: Solidago odora (Sweet Goldenrod)
DescriptionBase: Arching stems of yellow flowers sprout from basal clumps in late summer and early fall. Does not spread as aggressively as some other goldenrod species.   Drought resistant and clay tolerant.
Photo Credits: Dick Culbert from Gibsons, B.C., Canada, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, Eric Hunt, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Solidago rugosa (Wrinkle-leaved Solidago)
DescriptionBase: Rhizomatous plant may also self seed. Long spears of showy yellow flowers bloom in lat summer to fall, attracting bees and butterflies. Tolerates deer and clay soil.
Photo Credits: By Dominicus Johannes Bergsma - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=36222252, Rob Routledge, Sault College, Bugwood.org, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Solidago speciosa (Showy Goldenrod)
DescriptionBase: One of the most showy native goldenrods as their common name suggests. Tall upright stems host foot-long rods of golden yellow flowers in the early fall. Tolerates drought and clay soil.
Photo Credits: Dseiver, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Symphyotrichum cordifolium (Heart-Leaved Aster)
DescriptionBase: Leafy, upright-branched stems are topped with daisy-like, light-blue aster flowers in late summer and fall. Lower leaves are heart-shaped and up to 5" long. Attractive to butterflies.
Photo Credits: Cephas, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Symphyotrichum laeve (Smooth Blue Aster)
DescriptionBase: This aster tolerates drought and poor soils, and will reseed itself easily. Small (1") violet-blue flowers with yellow centers bloom in autumn and attract butterflies.

Item.Name: Symphyotrichum lateriflorum (Calico Aster)
DescriptionBase: Herbaceous perennial in the daisy family.  Blooms many white to pale lavendar flowers in late summer.  Light green to reddish brown hairy stems.
Photo Credits: Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons, Ryan Hodnett, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Symphyotrichum novae-angliae (New England Aster)
DescriptionBase: Late-blooming, showy aster that hosts a variety of butterflies, and provides a much needed food source during fall migration.
Photo Credits:
Jennifer Anderson. United States, IA, Scott Co., Davenport, Nahant Marsh. 2002. [Public domain]
The original uploader was Hugowolf at English Wikipedia. [CC BY-SA (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/)]

Item.Name: Symphyotrichum oblongifolium (Aromatic Aster)
DescriptionBase: Purple aster-flowers with yellow disks emerge in late summer, attracting birds and butterflies.. Tolerates sandy soils, poor soils, and drought.
Photo Credits: By PD-USGov-Interior-FWS - This image originates from the National Digital Library of the United States Fish and Wildlife Serviceat this pageThis tag does not indicate the copyright status of the attached work. A normal copyright tag is still required. See Commons:Licensing.See Category:Images from the United States Fish and Wildlife Service.Transferred from en.wikipedia to Commons.(Original text: http://www.fws.gov/digitalmedia/FullRes/natdiglib/IMG0016.jpg), Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=11516854, ShenandoahNPS, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Symphyotrichum pilosum (Frost Aster)
DescriptionBase: Noted for its extremely hairy stem and leaves. Flowers bloom in August to October. Best growth occurs in moist, semi-shaded woodland areas. Can tolerate periodic flooding. Can spread aggressively by self-spreading.
Photo Credits: By Joshua Mayer from Madison, WI, USA - Frost Aster (Symphyotrichum pilosum), CC BY-SA 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=97321598, Craig Martin, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Thalictrum dioicum (Early Meadow Rue)
DescriptionBase: Native perennial wildflower in the Buttercup family.  Lacy gray-green compound leaves.  Blooms tiny, drooping, greenish to white petal-less flowers in late spring (April-May).
Photo Credits: Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons,  Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons,  Ryan Hodnett, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Tiarella cordifolia (Creeping Foam Flower)
DescriptionBase: Perennial with glossy heart shaped leaves forms into dense clumps. Showy white flower clusters rise above the leaves in April and May and have a feathery appearance. Good woodland groundcover.
Photo Credits:
CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=127319 CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=127267, Wasrts, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
By Photo by David J. Stang - source: David Stang. First published at ZipcodeZoo.com, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=61189176

Item.Name: Tradescantia ohiensis (Ohio Spiderwort)
DescriptionBase: Clump forming perennial in the spiderwort family that blooms vibrant purple flowers with three petals in mid to late summer.  Slender blue-green leaves resemble grass blades.  Long bloom period.
Photo Credits: Cephas, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, KENPEI, CC BY-SA 3.0 <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Tradescantia virginiana (Virginia Spiderwort)
DescriptionBase: Violet-blue to purple, three-petaled flowers bloom in succession from May into early July. Tolerates clay and wet soil.
Photo Credits: By Fritzflohrreynolds - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=31468517, Concord, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Triadenum virginicum (Virginia Marsh St. John's Wort)
DescriptionBase: Small (1/2") pink to red, five-pointed flowers bloom in the summer in bogs, wet meadows, and swamps.
Photo Credits:
Ken-ichi Ueda [CC BY (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0)]
Fritz Flohr Reynolds [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0)]

Item.Name: Trillium grandiflorum (White Trillium)
DescriptionBase: Largest and showiest flower of all  trilliums! Flowers are 2 to 4 inches across and held above large glossy green bracts. Spreads slowly by rhizomes.
Photo Credits: Cbaile19, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Triodanis perfoliata (Venus' Looking Glass)
DescriptionBase: Small, unbranched plant with a light green, grooved stem. Blue or violet flowers emerge in the spring, supporting the Plasterer bee.
Photo Credits:
?? ?, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons; 
Melissa McMasters from Memphis, TN, United States, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Uvularia grandiflora (Bellwort)
DescriptionBase: Yellow flowers have long, twisted petals and bright green leaves. Plant is droopy in appearance. Excellent shade and border plant. Drought tolerant.
Photo Credits: Hardyplants at English Wikipedia [Public domain], Agnieszka Kwiecień, Nova, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Verbena hastata (Blue Vervain)
DescriptionBase: Delicate, blue-violet flowers sit atop stalks in mid summer, and can self-seed. They love prairies and rain gardens.
Photo Credits: Joshua Mayer from Madison, WI, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Verbena urticifolia (White Vervain)
DescriptionBase: Summer blooms of small, charming 1/8" white flowers on slender spikes are a nectar source for pollinators and insects. Seeds feed songbirds.
Photo Credits: Ryan Hodnett, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Vernonia gigantea (Giant Ironweed)
DescriptionBase: Upright flowering perennial with rose-purple composite flowers disks bloom from summer through fall. Durable plant. Does well in wet soil. Deer resistant.
Photo Credits: Greg Hume [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0)], Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Vernonia noveboracensis (New York Ironweed)
DescriptionBase: Striking purple flowers add color to damp roadsides and ponds from late summer to fall.
Photo Credits: Doug McGrady from Warwick, RI, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Veronicastrum virginicum (Black Root)
DescriptionBase: Tall herbaceous perennial with whorled lance shaped leaves and white to pale blue flowering racemes that bloom from late spring to summer.
Photo Credits: Acabashi, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  H. Zell, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Viola pedata (Bird's Foot Violet)
DescriptionBase: Small perennial Viola that spreads through seeds.   Unique bird-foot shaped foliage.  Often considered one of the most beautiful species of Violas.  Attracts bees, butterflies, mammals and songbirds.
Photo Credits: Eric in SF, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Joshua Mayer from Madison, WI, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Viola sororia (Common Blue Violet)
DescriptionBase: Herbaceous perennial that spreads through rhizomes and self seeding.  Prefers partial sun but will grow in most light conditions.  Produces showy blue, pink, purple and white flowers in early spring. Attracts bees and butterflies.
Photo Credits: Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons,   Famartin, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Yucca filamentosa (Adam's Needle)
DescriptionBase: Flowering stalk rises from center in late spring. White, showy, bell shaped flowers. Attracts hummingbirds, moths, small mammals, and songbirds. Only pollinated by Yucca Moths. Tolerant of poor, sandy soils, heat and drought. Will form a small colony over time from basal offsets.
Photo Credits: Maja Dumat, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Zizia aptera (Heart-leaf Golden Alexanders)
DescriptionBase: Small clusters of bright yellow flowers bloom in late spring, growing in colonies in open woodland and prairies. Perfect for cut flowers!
Photo Credits: Sara Kothari, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons By H. Zell - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=11503817

Item.Name: Zizia aurea (Golden Alexanders)
DescriptionBase: Flat-topped clusters of compound yellow flowers bloom in late spring. Attracts butterflies, as the larvae of the swallowtail feed on it.
Photo Credits: Photo by David J. Stang [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0)], H. Zell, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Andropogon gerardii (Big Bluestem)
DescriptionBase: Tall prairie grass with leaves and stems that change color with the seasons. Grows well in any soil. Deer and drought resistant.
Photo Credits: Matt Lavin from Bozeman, Montana, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Andropogon virginicus (Broom Sedge)
DescriptionBase: A clump forming grass that turns a golden orange in the fall and winter, creating spectacular views. Does well in poor soils and tolerates salt well.
Photo Credits: By Forest & Kim Starr, CC BY 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=6163753

Item.Name: Bouteloua curtipendula (Sideoats Grama)
