DescriptionBase: Distinctive oat-like seed spikes hang to the side of its flowering stems, with blueish-grey leaves. Grass turns gorgeous golds and oranges as it goes dormant each winter, creating lovely structure in your garden. Drought tolerant.
Photo Credits:
Tom Koerner/USFWS
Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=444116

Item.Name: Bromus latiglumis (Ear-leaved Brome)
DescriptionBase: Vibrant green cool season grass.  Clump forming- terrific habitat for birds and mammals.
Photo Credits: Reuven Martin, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Calamagrostis canadensis (Bluejoint Reedgrass)
DescriptionBase: Thriving in wet areas, this bunchgrass is also drought tolerant. Grows rapidly to fill a desired space.
Photo Credits: Superior National Forest, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Carex amphibola (Creek Sedge)
DescriptionBase: Vigorous clump forming sedge with narrow, arching leaves. Good for shade and rain gardens. Deer and pest resistant. Attracts skipper butterflies.
Photo Credits: Photo by Doug McGreedy

Item.Name: Carex appalachica (Appalachian Sedge)
DescriptionBase: Weeping sedge with hair like foliage grows in attractive mounded tufts. Can be used as ground cover or replacement for grass. Adaptable to difficult dry areas. Deer tolerant.

Item.Name: Carex blanda (Eastern Woodland Sedge)
DescriptionBase: This evergreen sedge displays small flower-spikes in late spring. Drought tolerant. Evergreen
Photo Credits: Alison Northup, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Carex eburnea (Bristle-Leaved Sedge)
DescriptionBase: Soft thread-like leaves form spherical porcupine-like clumps. Will thrive with consistent moisture but can adapt to sandy or rocky soils.
Photo Credits:
By Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=76394287
By Reuven Martin - https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/25533727, CC0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=87489679

Item.Name: Carex flaccosperma (Blue Wood Sedge)
DescriptionBase: Evergreen sedge with arching blue-green leaves forms slowing spreading clumps. Grows best in evenly moist woodland areas with bright shade but will take some sun.
Photo Credits: By Choess - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=21911967, DouglasGoldman, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Carex pensylvanica (Pennsylvania Sedge)
DescriptionBase: This soft, graceful ground cover is semi-evergreen in milder climates. It can be used as a lawn alternative but does not tolerate foot traffic.
Photo Credits: By Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=52326399 By Photo by David J. Stang - source: David Stang. First published at ZipcodeZoo.com, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=61092351

Item.Name: Carex plantaginea (Broad leafed Sedge)
DescriptionBase: Herbaceous perennial sedge with broad plantain-like leaves and brown to purple blooms.

Item.Name: Carex radiata (Eastern Star Sedge)
DescriptionBase: Evergreen clump growing sedge. Grass-like medium blades. Tolerates wet soil.
Photo Credits: By Daderot - Own work, CC0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=28311200

Item.Name: Carex vulpinoidea (Fox Sedge)
DescriptionBase: Narrow grass-like leaves grow in clumps in wet soils. Seedheads spread out from the center of the clump, resembling a fox tail. Can spread quickly. Deer tolerant.
Photo Credits: Jay Sturner from USA [CC BY (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0)]

Item.Name: Chasmanthium latifolium (River Oats)
DescriptionBase: Shade tolerant, aggressive spreading grass. Characterized by striking flat, drooping seed heads that flutter in the breeze and turn bronze late summer.
Photo Credits: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Danthonia spicata (Poverty Oat Grass)
DescriptionBase: Short bunchgrass perennial with rosettes of curly leaves.  Flowering stems can be up to two feet high but base of rosett stay close to the ground.  Can grow in dry, rocky and sandy soils.
Photo Credits: Rob Routledge, Sault College, Bugwood.org, CC BY 3.0 US <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/us/deed.en>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Rob Routledge, Sault College, Bugwood.org, CC BY 3.0 US <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/us/deed.en>, via Wikimedia Commons,

Item.Name: Elymus hystrix (Bottlebrush Grass)
DescriptionBase: Loose upright tufts of narrow-bladed grass. Greenish flower heads, resembling bottle brushes, rise in the summer. Can tolerate drought and clay tolerant.
Photo Credits: wackybadger, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Elymus virginicus (Wild Rye)
DescriptionBase: A shorter bunchgrass that tolerates drought, deer, and erosion. Does will in a wide variety of soils too!
Photo Credits: Matt Lavin from Bozeman, Montana, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Eragrostis spectabilis (Purple Lovegrass)
DescriptionBase: Hardy, native perennial with seed heads that turn purple mid-summer, giving color to the landscape.
Photo Credits: Photo by David J. Stang, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Muhlenbergia capillaris (Pink Muhlygrass)
DescriptionBase: Best in full sun, this clump-grass is known for its beautiful, pink foliage that blooms in foot-long spires in fall. Drought tolerant.
Photo Credits:
Heather Paul: https://www.flickr.com/photos/warriorwoman531/8154947318, Photo by David J. Stang, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
John Winder: https://www.flickr.com/photos/83833206@N00/8247811269

Item.Name: Panicum virgatum (Switchgrass)
DescriptionBase: Clump grass topped with pink-tinged flower panicles that rise over the grass and are a source of food for birds in the winter. Drought and flood tolerant.
Photo Credits: Photo by David J. Stang [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0)]\, Photo by David J. Stang, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Schizachyrium scoparium (Little Bluestem)
DescriptionBase: This small bunchgrass creates clusters of fluffy seed pods that add great interest even through the winter. Tolerates clay, dry, and rocky soils.
Photo Credits: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz [CC BY-SA (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0)], Joshua Mayer from Madison, WI, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Scirpus atrovirens (Dark Green Bulrush)
DescriptionBase: This large rush can grow in standing water, making it excellent for the edges of ponds. Spreads rhizomously to form colonies.
Photo Credits: By - https://identify.plantnet.org/the-plant-list/species/Scirpus%20atrovirens%20Willd./data

Item.Name: Scirpus cyperinus (Woolgrass)
DescriptionBase: Thrives easily in wet, standing water, making this ideal for ponds. Can colonize an area as well. Reddish brown, wooly seed clusters emerge in late summer, remaining throughout the winter.
Photo Credits:
Fredlyfish4, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons
Ryan Hodnett, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Sorghastrum nutans (Indian Grass)
DescriptionBase: Tall warm season bunchgrass that blooms yellow in late summer.  Foliage turns a beautiful yellow-orange in fall. Provides food and cover year round for wildlife especially birds and mammals. Tolerant to a wide variety of soil types.
Photo Credits: USFWS Mountain Prairie, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons, Joshua Mayer from Madison, WI, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Sporobolus heterolepis (Prairie Dropseed)
DescriptionBase: This slow-growing, clump forming grass gains orange hues in the fall and retains much of its shape and appearance in through the winter. Flowers have a unique scent. Drought and clay tolerant.
Photo Credits: By Photo by David J. Stang - source: David Stang. First published at ZipcodeZoo.com, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=61094570

Item.Name: Tridens flavus (Purpletop)
DescriptionBase: Purplelish spikelets on stalks that, then planted in groups, creates a beautiful purple-topped field. Grass is home to many native insect species and seed feeds birds. Drought tolerant.
Photo Credits: Mary Keim https://www.flickr.com/photos/38514062@N03/8041397283/

Common Name: False Indigo Bush or River Locust
Item.Name: Amorpha fruticosa (False Indigo Bush or River Locust)
DescriptionBase: Tubular, scented flowers bloom in May-June in spiked clusters, followed by small seed pods in July and August. Good for moist areas or poor soils. Attracts Butterflies.
Photo Credits: Dalgial, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Amorpha nana (Fragrant False Indigo)
DescriptionBase: Bright green leaves and showy, upright purple flowers that emerge in summer. Deer resistant. Native to West of MS, not VA. We will sell until out and stop carrying it.
Photo Credits: By Denis.prévôt - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=15861840

Item.Name: Eubotrys racemosa (Swamp Doghobble)
DescriptionBase: deciduous shrub with shiny green and red stems. White bell-shaped flowers on 3-4' racemes in spring that give way to light brown fruits and red leaves in fall.
Photo Credits: Famartin, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Kalmia angustifolia (Sheep Laurel)
DescriptionBase: Evergreen shrub with groups of round, pink flowers in summer. Can spread to form colonies. Does well at bog or water edges. Host for the Brown Elfin butterfly.
Photo Credits:
Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons
Ryan Hodnett, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Prunus angustifolia (Chickasaw Plum)
DescriptionBase: Tall shrub.  Smooth redish bark with numerous  light lenticels. Bark becomes scaly and shallowly furrowed as it ages. Blooms small white flowers in clumps tht give way to edible yellow-to-red drupes that mature in summer.
Photo Credits: Lm13700, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, Homer Edward Price, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Rhododendron arborescens (Sweet Azalea)
DescriptionBase: Leaves turn red and purple in the fall. Fragrant, funnel-shaped white flowers bloom in groups of 3-6 early summer with protruding stamen.
Photo Credits: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons,  Fritzflohrreynolds, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Robinia hispida (Bristly Locust)
DescriptionBase: Rose to pale purple flowers appear in May, followed by bristly, purple seed pods. Plant is stiffly hair. Tolerates dry, poor soils and Deer. Aggressive spreader.
Photo Credits: Ivana Kruljević, CC BY 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Rosa carolina (Pasture Rose)
DescriptionBase: Can spread to form colonies. Single, pink flowers bloom in may with red hips in the late summer. Attracts birds and butterflies
Photo Credits: Salicyna, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Rosa palustris (Swamp Rose)
DescriptionBase: Pink blooms in the summer characterize this swamp plant, making it an excellent choice for wet, sunny conditions. Tolerates seasonal flooding.
Photo Credits:
Fritz Flohr Reynolds, CC BY-SA 2.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, mmmavocado, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
I, SB Johnny, CC BY-SA 3.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Rubus odoratus (Purple Flowering Raspberry)
DescriptionBase: Deciduous suckering shrub with large canes.  One of the most easily identifiable rubus species due to their gorgeous palmately lobed leaves, hairy stems and pink-purple rose shaped flowers that bloom in Spring and Summer. Edible fruits mature in summer. Attracts bees, butterflies, birds and small mammals
Photo Credits: Ryan Hodnett, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,    Ryan Hodnett, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia,   Commons, Anneli Salo, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Salix discolor (Pussy Willow)
DescriptionBase: Large multi stemmed dioecious shrub.  Male shrubs bloom silky gray flowers in spring before leaves emerge.  Females produce smaller green catkins.  Rapid spreading to form thickets. Tolerant of severe pruning after flowering.
Photo Credits: Silk666, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   Famartin, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,

Item.Name: Spiraea latifolia (Meadowsweet)
DescriptionBase: Whitish pink blooms in summer. Showy flowers attract butterflies and fruits attract birds in fall. Needs moist soils.

Item.Name: Spiraea tomentosa (Steeplebush)
DescriptionBase: Upright, multi-stemmed, suckering, deciduous shrub.  Bloom tiny pink, rose-purple flowers on terminal spikes that from mid-summer to early autumn.  Attract butterflies.
Photo Credits: Eric Hunt, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, Doug McGrady from Warwick, RI, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Symphoricarpos albus (Common Snowberry)
DescriptionBase: Dense suckering deciduous shrub. Attractive bright green leaves.  Pink to white flowers in spring and summer are followed by attractive white fruits that last into winter.  Forms colonies but needs more than one genetic strain to fruit.
Photo Credits: Muséum de Toulouse, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   korina.info, korina.info – CC-BY-SA-4.0, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Rudolphous, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,   Joe Mabel, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Viburnum acerifolium (Mapleleaf Viburnum)
DescriptionBase: Small, rounded shrub with maple-like leaves. Tiny white flowers bloom in mid spring and blueish-black fruit in late summer. More shade tolerant than many viburnums. Established plants are drought tolerant. They will sucker to form colonies.
Photo Credits: Plant Image Library, CC BY-SA 2.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, Aaron Carlson, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Viburnum trilobum (Cranberry Viburnum)
DescriptionBase: Large hardy shrub blooms white flower clusters in spring. Dark green leaves turn a purple-red in fall along with bright red berries appearing. Edible fruit for humans and birds.
Photo Credits: Cephas, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Common Name: STICKER: Scarlett Bee Balm w/ bumblebees
Item.Name: STICKER: Scarlett Bee Balm w/ bumblebees
Aesculus parviflora (Bottlebrush Buckeye)
DescriptionBase: Delightful summer blooms of showy, 12" flower clusters of tubular white flowers and bright red anthers. Flowers give way to pear-shaped buckeyes and foliage turns yellow in autumn. Drought and Deer tolerant. Attracts Butterflies
Photo Credits: Wendy Cutler from Vancouver, Canada [CC BY (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0)]

Item.Name: Aesculus pavia (Red Eastern Buckeye)
DescriptionBase: Bright, red, upright flower clusters bloom in spring, which attract hummingbirds. Buckeyes ripen in the fall. Prefers some afternoon or extra moisture in hot, dry conditions.
Photo Credits:
Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons
Betty from Eads, Tennessee, United States, CC BY 2.0 , via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Betula papyrifera (Paper Birch)
DescriptionBase: Quick growing single or multi-stemmed tree noted for its peeling white bark that reveals an orange-brown inner bark. Mature trees develop black markings on the white bark. Produce copper-green catkins in spring. Yellow leaves in fall.
Photo Credits: daryl_mitchell from Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, Canada, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons , English: NPS, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons, Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Betula populifolia (Grey Birch)
DescriptionBase: Deciduous suckering tree that often appears in a multi-trunked form, but can also grow with a single trunk. Distinguishable by its chalky white bark.  Yellowish-brown male flowers in single catkins  and greenish female flowers in smaller upright catkins in spring.
Photo Credits: Nicholas A. Tonelli from Northeast Pennsylvania, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, DouglasGoldman, CC BY-SA 4.0 , Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons<https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  DouglasGoldman, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Castanea dentata (American Chestnut)
DescriptionBase: Despite problems with blight, this tree can easily grow to 35', providing habitat and food for birds and insects. It also shoots out smaller trees that will establish even after the main tree suffers.
Photo Credits: Agnieszka Kwiecień, Nova, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Franklinia alatamaha (Franklin Tree)
DescriptionBase: Camellia-like, cup-shaped, 5-petaled, sweetly-fragrant, white flowers (to 3� diameter) bloom in late summer.� Leaves turn quality shades of orange, red and purple in autumn. Sole species in genus. Found by W. Bartram in 1765 along Georgia's Altamaha River. All Franklinia growing today allegedly descend from those propagated and distributed by the Bartrams, who are often credited with having saved the tree from extinction. Note on Nativity: This is a North American Native plant with original range in GEORGIA, USA. We carry it because it is special.
Photo Credits: Plant Image Library from Boston, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Gymnocladus dioicus (Kentucky Coffeetree)
DescriptionBase: Deciduous tree in the Fabaceae family.  Crown grows in an oval, open, to irregular habit.   Large pinnately compound leaves that emerge in the late spring as pinkish-bronze and then mature to a bluish-green. Greenish-white flowers appear in late spring to early summer. Leaves turn yellow and female trees boast clusters of large purplish-brown  seed pods in fall.
Photo Credits: Bostonian13, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Leonora (Ellie) Enking from East Preston, United Kingdom, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, ??????? ???????? / Dimětar Nŕydenov, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,

Item.Name: Magnolia tripetala (Umbrella Magnolia)
DescriptionBase: Medium-sized tree that blooms with fragrant cup-shaped white flowers in spring. Red fruits mature in summer. This tree gets its name from its large leaves that cluster at the ends of its stems.

Item.Name: Malus coronaria American Crabapple)
DescriptionBase: Small native tree in the Rose family blooms fragrant, light pink flowers in spring.  Small fruits that can be made into preserves or cider

Item.Name: Prunus maritima (Beach Plum)
DescriptionBase: White flowers in spring give way to dull 1" purple, edible fruits.
Photo Credits: Plant Image Library from Boston, USA, CC BY-SA 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Quercus macrocarpa (Bur Oak)
DescriptionBase: The massive Bur Oak can adapt to a wide range of soils. Acorns give name to the tree with the bur near the rim.
Photo Credits:
By Daderot - Own work, CC0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=23478883, Laval University, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons
Matt Lavin: https://www.flickr.com/photos/plant_diversity/5108085936

Item.Name: Quercus prinoides (Dwarf Chestnut Oak)
DescriptionBase: Deciduous oak tree blooms with green and yellow catkins in spring.  Leaves turn green and copper in the fall.  Matures quickly.  Tolerant of poor, dry soils.

Item.Name: Quercus prinus (Chestnut Oak)
DescriptionBase: Large native deciduous tree in the white oak family.  Often found growing in poor, dry and rocky soils.  Does best in well-drained loams. Separate male and female flowers appear in spring.  Large acorns produced in fall that have wildlife value for birds and mammals. Frequently used as a shade tree.
Photo Credits: Bruce Kirchoff from Greensboro, NC, USA, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Douglas Goldman, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Douglas Goldman, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Aristolochia tomentosa (Wooly Dutchmans Pipe)
DescriptionBase: Deciduous, woody vine with hairy, heart-shaped leaves.  Blooms unique yellow and purple flowers in spring and summer. Native to TN & WV; Not native to VA

Item.Name: Clematis occidentalis var. occidentalis (Purple Clematis)
DescriptionBase: Trailing vine that blooms lavender purple to pinkish grey showy flowers.
Photo Credits: Thayne Tuason, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons,  Thayne Tuason, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service - Midwest Region, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Clematis virginiana (Virgin's Bower)
DescriptionBase: This vine blooms can grow to 20' tall with support; without it will spread as a ground cover. White flowers in spires are fragrant and bloom in fall which give way to plumes of seed heads. Can spread aggressively.
Photo Credits: R. A. Nonenmacher, CC BY-SA 4.0 , via Wikimedia Commons, Cbaile19, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Lonicera sempervirens (Coral Honeysuckle)
DescriptionBase: This vine creates beautiful red, cone-shaped flowers tipped with orange bloom in spring in small clusters that attract hummingbirds and butterflies. Tolerates clay soil.
Photo Credits: Eric Hunt, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

Item.Name: Passiflora incarnata (Purple Passion Flower)
DescriptionBase: Fast growing climbing vine blooms fragrant showy flowers in the summer. Roots appreciate loose mulch and spread by root suckers. Edible fruit appears in July and mature in fall where it can be eaten off vine or made into jelly. Drought tolerant.
